content.text: Where you run your LLMs determines cost structure, data governance, operational complexity, and the upper bound on request throughput. Neither cloud nor on-premises is universally superior — the right answer depends on utilisation rate, team size, compliance requirements, and growth trajectory.

The core financial question is whether your GPU utilisation rate is high enough to justify CapEx. At low utilisation, cloud's pay-per-use model wins. Above a crossover point — typically around 60–70% sustained GPU utilisation — owning hardware becomes cheaper.
is annual power cost per GPU, and
is annualised MLOps headcount allocated to hardware operations.

def tco_cloud(num_gpus: int, utilisation_pct: float, hrs_per_year: int = 8760,
              hourly_rate: float = 4.0) -> dict:
    """Estimate annual cloud cost (e.g., A100 80GB at ~$4/hr per GPU)."""
    billed_hrs = hrs_per_year * (utilisation_pct / 100)
    annual_cost = num_gpus * billed_hrs * hourly_rate
    return {"annual_$": round(annual_cost), "per_gpu_$": round(annual_cost / num_gpus)}

def tco_onprem(num_gpus: int, hw_cost_per_gpu: float = 25_000,
               power_kwh_per_gpu_yr: float = 4_380,  # 500W * 8760h
               elec_rate: float = 0.10,
               staff_cost_yr: float = 180_000,
               staff_gpu_ratio: float = 64,
               hw_life_yrs: float = 4.0) -> dict:
    hw_amortised = (num_gpus * hw_cost_per_gpu) / hw_life_yrs
    power_cost   = num_gpus * power_kwh_per_gpu_yr * elec_rate
    staff_cost   = (num_gpus / staff_gpu_ratio) * staff_cost_yr
    annual_cost  = hw_amortised + power_cost + staff_cost
    return {"annual_$": round(annual_cost), "per_gpu_$": round(annual_cost / num_gpus)}

# Example: 32 A100s
cloud = tco_cloud(32, utilisation_pct=70, hourly_rate=3.21)   # A100 80GB spot
onprem = tco_onprem(32)

print(f"Cloud  (70% util): ${cloud['annual_$']:,}/yr  (${cloud['per_gpu_$']:,}/GPU/yr)")
print(f"On-prem:           ${onprem['annual_$']:,}/yr  (${onprem['per_gpu_$']:,}/GPU/yr)")
# Typical output:
# Cloud  (70% util): $630,978/yr  ($19,718/GPU/yr)
# On-prem:           $427,600/yr  ($13,363/GPU/yr)

The on-prem model wins at 70% utilisation, but the $800K upfront purchase is a separate budget decision. At 30% utilisation, cloud costs roughly half as much annually because you only pay for active hours.

Transferring training data from S3/GCS to a training cluster costs $0.08–0.09/GB in most regions. A 1 TB dataset re-loaded 50 times per year costs ~$4,000 in egress alone. Keep data and compute in the same cloud region, or use object storage with no-egress-fee zones (GCS Coldline within region is free for internal reads).

Spot A100s cost 60–70% less than on-demand but can be preempted with 2 minutes notice. Checkpoint overhead (10–20 min to save, reload a 70B model) must be weighed against savings. Spot instances suit fine-tuning with frequent checkpoints, not inference serving.

1-year reserved A100 instances (AWS p4d) cost ~$24/hr vs ~$33/hr on-demand — 27% savings. 3-year reservations are ~40% off. Reserved capacity is appropriate if utilisation is predictable 6+ months out.
AWS Enterprise Support ($15,000/mo minimum) and Azure Unified ($50,000/yr) add materially to cloud TCO for production AI workloads.

Regulated industries (healthcare, finance, government) often have explicit requirements that patient data, financial records, or classified information must not transit public cloud infrastructure. These requirements drive on-premises decisions independent of cost.
BAA required; shared tenancy risk for PHI
Air-gapped GPU cluster, no PHI egress
Data transfer to US requires SCCs or Adequacy
Full control of audit scope boundary
Allowed on cloud with CDE isolation
(Azure) that eliminate shared-hypervisor concerns. These cost 2–3× standard instances but may satisfy compliance requirements while retaining cloud operational benefits.

(RDMA over Ethernet). Cloud clusters typically use proprietary backends (AWS EFA at 3.2 Tb/s for p4d/p5, Google's Jupiter fabric, Azure InfiniBand for HBv3) that approximate InfiniBand performance but with higher tail latency.

# Measure effective all-reduce bandwidth in your environment
# NCCL benchmark (run on each node in the cluster)
# git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/nccl-tests && cd nccl-tests && make MPI=1

# Run from rank 0:
# mpirun -np 8 --hostfile hosts ./build/all_reduce_perf -b 1G -e 8G -f 2 -g 1

# Typical results (8× A100 NVLink within a node):
#   Size(B)    BW(GB/s)   AlgBW    BusBW
#   1073741824  243.87    243.87   426.78

# Compare across AWS p4d (EFA), on-prem IB HDR, on-prem RoCE:
# EFA:    ~200 GB/s intra-node, 40–60 GB/s inter-node
# IB HDR: ~200 GB/s intra-node, 150–180 GB/s inter-node
# RoCE:   ~200 GB/s intra-node, 80–120 GB/s inter-node

, the relevant latency is the round-trip from the application server to the GPU cluster. Cloud co-location within a region adds ~0.5–2 ms vs a local on-prem deployment. For streaming generation this is negligible. For high-volume, low-latency APIs (e.g., code autocomplete), co-locating app servers with GPUs in the same on-prem rack cuts p99 TTFT by 3–5 ms.
Cloud's elastic scaling suits workloads with sharp traffic peaks — diurnal patterns, product launches, batch jobs. A managed autoscaling group can add GPU instances within 2–5 minutes:

On-premises capacity must be provisioned ahead of demand. The planning horizon includes procurement lead time (NVIDIA H100: 3–9 months in 2023–2024), rack installation (2–4 weeks), and software commissioning (1–2 weeks). Typical on-prem scaling strategies:
Buy in waves (e.g., 32 GPUs every 6 months) to match demand growth and avoid a single large obsolescence event.

Keep steady-state on-prem but overflow spikes to cloud. A Kubernetes cluster with on-prem nodes as the default scheduler pool and cloud nodes as a secondary pool (cluster autoscaler + Karpenter) implements this automatically.
Rent rack space in a Tier III/IV data centre with your own hardware. Eliminates facility CapEx (power, cooling, physical security) while retaining hardware ownership.
Most mature ML platforms run a hybrid model: on-prem for steady-state production inference and training, cloud for burst capacity, experimentation, and disaster recovery.

# Kubernetes hybrid cluster (on-prem + cloud burst)
# Node labels identify location for scheduling
#
# On-prem nodes (always available):
#   kubectl label node gpu-node-01 topology=on-prem workload=inference
#   kubectl label node gpu-node-02 topology=on-prem workload=inference
#
# Cloud nodes (burst via Karpenter or cluster-autoscaler):
#   NodeClass → g5.xlarge, p3.2xlarge (spot)
#   topology=cloud label applied automatically

# Pod spec: prefer on-prem, allow cloud fallback
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        - weight: 100
          preference:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: topology
                operator: In
                values: ["on-prem"]
  tolerations:
    - key: "cloud-node"
      operator: "Exists"
      effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
    - name: llm-server
      image: your-registry/vllm-server:latest
      resources:
        limits:
          nvidia.com/gpu: 1
This configuration schedules inference workloads on on-prem nodes with GPU cost near zero (already owned), and spills overflow to cloud nodes when on-prem capacity is exhausted.
Second site or cloud DR required
Use this checklist to guide the initial deployment decision:
Estimate annual GPU-hours needed. Below ~50,000 GPU-hours/yr (roughly 6 A100s at 95% util), cloud is almost always cheaper. Above ~200,000 GPU-hours/yr, on-prem or colo pays off within 18 months.
Identify data classification level and applicable regulations. If any data is Restricted/PHI/PCI with physical-control requirements, start with on-prem or dedicated-host cloud.
On-prem requires at least 1 FTE per ~64 GPUs for hardware operations, plus network engineering. Teams under ~10 engineers should lean cloud until operational maturity is established.
Cloud gives GPU access same day. On-prem takes 3–9 months from PO to production. If you're in early experimentation, cloud.
Track GPU utilisation weekly for 3 months. If it exceeds 65% consistently, model on-prem TCO. If it fluctuates below 40%, cloud remains optimal.
If training data is already in a cloud object store (petabytes), moving it to on-prem costs more than it saves. Compute follows data.

Use spot with checkpoint-every-N-steps. For a 70B fine-tune taking 40 hours, spot saves ~65% ($200 vs $580 on g5.48xlarge). Implement automatic restart via SageMaker Managed Spot Training or a custom fault-tolerant launcher.
AWS Compute Savings Plans and GCP CUDs discount at 40–66% for 1–3 year commitments. Apply to inference workloads with stable throughput.
Profile model VRAM usage. A 7B FP16 model fits on a single A10G (24 GB) — no need to pay for an A100. Use
to measure peak memory per request.
INT4 AWQ or GPTQ reduces 70B model from 140 GB to 35–40 GB, enabling a 2-GPU A100 deployment instead of 4 GPUs. Cloud cost halved.

Data centre PUE (total facility power / IT power) multiplies your effective electricity cost. A PUE of 1.4 turns $0.10/kWh into $0.14/kWh effective. Colocations typically run PUE 1.2–1.4; purpose-built ML facilities can reach 1.05.

Run batch training jobs on GPUs idle from inference during off-hours. A scheduler (SLURM, Kubernetes gang scheduling) can backfill overnight training onto GPUs that serve inference during business hours.
After 3–4 years, A100s retain ~30–50% of new value on the secondary market. Factor resale value into 4-year TCO calculations.

Cloud wins on agility, operational simplicity, and low-utilisation economics; on-premises wins on sustained high-utilisation TCO, data sovereignty, and network predictability. The crossover is roughly 60–70% GPU utilisation over 12+ months. Most production ML platforms converge on a hybrid model: on-prem for steady-state load, cloud for burst and disaster recovery. Start cloud, migrate workloads on-prem as utilisation validates the capital investment.
